lesson.title: ACF, PACF & Model Identification
lesson.body: The sample ACF estimates ρ(h) from data and is the primary tool for detecting serial correlation. The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) measures the correlation between x_t and x_{t-h} after removing the linear influence of all intermediate lags — it isolates the direct effect. The Ljung-Box test statistic Q = n(n+2) Σ_{k=1}^{m} ρ̂(k)²/(n-k) tests the null hypothesis that the first m autocorrelations are jointly zero; a significant Q indicates remaining structure. For model identification: a pure AR(p) has a PACF that cuts off after lag p and an ACF that tails off; a pure MA(q) has an ACF that cuts off after lag q and a PACF that tails off.

rooms.question: The PACF at lag h measures:
steps: Think: partial means we remove the effect of lags 1 through h-1.
answers: The correlation between x_t and x_{t-h} after controlling for x_{t-1}, ..., x_{t-h+1}
The total correlation between x_t and x_{t-h}
The correlation between consecutive first differences
The spectral density at frequency h
rooms.explanation: The PACF isolates the direct linear relationship between x_t and x_{t-h} by removing the indirect effects of all intermediate lags. This is what makes it useful for identifying the order p of AR models — the PACF cuts off sharply at lag p.

rooms.question: For an MA(2) process, what pattern do the ACF and PACF exhibit?
steps: MA(q): ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF tails off.
answers: ACF cuts off after lag 2; PACF tails off (decays gradually)
ACF tails off; PACF cuts off after lag 2
Both ACF and PACF cut off after lag 2
Both ACF and PACF tail off indefinitely
rooms.explanation: MA(q) processes have ACF that is exactly zero beyond lag q (cuts off) and a PACF that decays to zero (tails off). This is the signature used to identify MA order. The opposite pattern — PACF cuts off, ACF tails off — identifies AR order.

rooms.question: The Ljung-Box test with m = 10 lags gives Q = 18.3 (χ²₁₀ critical value at 5% is 18.31). What do you conclude?
steps: Compare Q to the critical value. H₀: all ρ(1),...,ρ(m) = 0.
answers: Marginally fail to reject H₀; no strong evidence of autocorrelation at 5% significance
Strongly reject H₀; the residuals are definitely autocorrelated
Accept H₀; the series is white noise
The test is invalid without knowing the sample size
rooms.explanation: Q = 18.3 is just below the 5% critical value of 18.31, so we marginally fail to reject H₀ at 5%. We cannot 'accept' H₀ in the statistical sense — we only fail to reject it. The result is borderline and warrants further inspection.

rooms.question: For identifying ARMA model order using ACF/PACF, match each pattern to its model. Order from AR-only to MA-only:
items: MA(q): ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF tails off
AR(p): PACF cuts off at lag p, ACF tails off
ARMA(p,q): both ACF and PACF tail off
White noise: both ACF and PACF are zero for all h > 0
rooms.explanation: White noise has no structure. AR(p) shows a PACF that cuts off and ACF that tails off. ARMA(p,q) shows both ACF and PACF tailing off — neither cuts off cleanly. MA(q) shows ACF cutting off and PACF tailing off. This ordering goes from pure AR to pure MA with mixed ARMA in between.

rooms.question: You fit an ARMA model and plot the residual ACF. What is the ideal outcome that confirms adequate fit?
steps: A good model should leave no exploitable structure in the residuals.
answers: Residual ACF is within ±1.96/√n bands for all lags — consistent with white noise
Residual ACF decays slowly to zero
Residual ACF is positive for all lags
Residual PACF cuts off at the AR order
rooms.explanation: If the model is adequate, residuals should resemble white noise. The 95% confidence band for a white noise ACF is approximately ±1.96/√n. If all sample autocorrelations fall within these bands, there is no evidence of remaining structure.

boss.name: The Autocorrelation Oracle
boss.taunt: Every lag reveals a secret about the past. Can you read the correlogram, or will the pattern consume you?

questions.question: An AR(3) process has which theoretical PACF pattern?
answers: PACF is non-zero at lags 1, 2, 3 and exactly zero for all lags h > 3
PACF decays exponentially to zero for all lags
PACF is zero at all lags
PACF cuts off after lag 1 regardless of the order
questions.explanation: For AR(p), the PACF cuts off sharply after lag p. An AR(3) has non-zero PACF at lags 1, 2, 3 and zero PACF beyond that. The ACF of an AR(p) tails off gradually, showing the distinction between the two functions.

questions.question: The sample ACF at lag 1 is ρ̂(1) = 0.72 with n = 100. Approximate 95% confidence band is ±1.96/√100 = ±0.196. What can you say?
answers: ρ̂(1) is significantly different from zero; there is strong evidence of lag-1 autocorrelation
ρ̂(1) is inside the confidence band so we cannot reject zero autocorrelation
The test requires at least 1000 observations to be valid
ρ̂(1) = 0.72 means the series is definitely AR(1)
questions.explanation: 0.72 >> 0.196, so ρ̂(1) is far outside the 95% band. We strongly reject H₀: ρ(1) = 0. Significant autocorrelation at lag 1 does not uniquely identify the model as AR(1) — it could be AR(p), MA(q), or ARMA(p,q).

questions.question: You observe that the sample ACF has significant spikes at lags 1 and 2, and the sample PACF decays slowly with spikes at many lags. Which model class is most consistent with this pattern?
answers: MA(2): ACF cuts off after lag 2, PACF tails off
AR(2): PACF cuts off after lag 2, ACF tails off
White noise: no significant lags in either
I(1) random walk: ACF decays very slowly
questions.explanation: ACF cutting off after lag 2 with a slowly decaying PACF is the signature of MA(2). AR(2) would show the opposite: PACF cutting off at lag 2 and ACF decaying slowly. ARMA processes show both tailing off rather than either cutting off.
